cards.body: You now know the whole family: Lola and Lolo at the top, Nanay and Tatay, Tita and Tito on the sides, Ate and Kuya above you, and you might be the Bunso. Every person has a title, and every title carries respect.

cards.explanation: Nanay = mom, Lolo = grandpa, Bunso = youngest, Ninang = godmother.

cards.prompt: Arrive at Lola's house. Greet her fully and respectfully.
cards.explanation: Mano po, Lola! Kumusta po kayo?

cards.source_text: Your nanay's best friend (her age) visits.
cards.explanation: Tita for any woman your parents' age.

cards.body: Notice the pattern: Nanay ko, kapatid mo, bahay ko, pamilya mo. Ko (my) and mo (your) work with everything. Family, things, places.

cards.source_text: When do you say mano po?
options: With any elder: Lola, Lolo, Tita, Tito, Ninong, Ninang
cards.explanation: Mano po for all elders when greeting them.
Mahal ko ang pamilya ko = I love my family.

cards.source_text: A cashier is a woman a few years older than you.
cards.prompt: You get her attention by saying:
cards.explanation: Ate for any woman slightly older. Tita would be for your parents' generation.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: You just learned 22 family-related words. In a Filipino family gathering, you can now greet everyone correctly, use mano po, and even say 'mahal kita.'
The Filipino family system has no real equivalent in Western culture. Ninong, ninang, and even close family friends become real family with real obligations.
'Pamilya' from Spanish, 'ate/kuya' from Hokkien Chinese, 'mano' from Spanish, but 'nanay,' 'tatay,' 'anak,' 'kapatid,' 'lola,' and 'lolo' are all pure Tagalog.
The ko/mo pattern you learned (nanay ko, tatay mo) is the foundation for possessives in Tagalog. Everything works the same way going forward.
